abandon: To leave behind or give up completely.
abate: To reduce in degree or intensity.
abbreviate: To shorten a word or phrase.
abdicate: To renounce one's throne or position.
abduct: To take someone away by force or threat.
abhor: To regard with disgust and hatred.
abide: To accept or act in accordance with.
ablate: To remove or destroy by melting or vaporizing.
abnormal: Deviating from what is normal or usual.
abolish: To formally put an end to a system or practice.
abominable: Causing moral revulsion; very bad or unpleasant.
aboriginal: Relating to the original inhabitants of a region.
abortive: Failing to produce the intended result.
abrasive: Showing little concern for the feelings of others.
abrogate: To repeal or do away with a law or agreement.
absence: The state of being absent or not present.
absent: Not present in a place or at an event.
absolute: Not qualified or diminished in any way.
absolve: To declare someone free from guilt or responsibility.
absorb: To take in or soak up.
abstain: To deliberately avoid using or participating in.
abstract: Existing in thought or as an idea but not having a physical existence.
abundance: A very large quantity of something.
abundant: Existing or occurring in large amounts.
abuse: To treat with cruelty or violence.
academy: A place of study or training in a special field.
accentuate: To make more noticeable or prominent.
accept: To consent to receive or undertake.
acceptable: Able to be agreed on; suitable.
acceptance: The action of consenting to receive or undertake something.
access: The means or opportunity to approach or reach.
accessible: Able to be reached or entered.
accessory: A thing that can be added to something else to make it more useful or attractive.
accident: An unfortunate incident that happens unexpectedly.
acclimate: To become accustomed to a new climate or environment.
accommodate: To provide lodging or sufficient space for.
account: A report or description of an event or experience.
accuse: To charge someone with an offense or crime.
accustom: To make someone or something familiar with something.
acerbic: Sharp and forthright in tone or manner.
acquaint: To make someone aware of or familiar with.
acquit: To free someone from a criminal charge by a verdict of not guilty.
acronym: An abbreviation formed from the initial letters of other words.
across: From one side to the other side.
activate: To make something active or operative.
adapt: To make something suitable for a new use or purpose.
add: To join something to something else.
addition: The action or process of adding something.
address: The particulars of the place where someone lives or an organization is situated.
adequate: Satisfactory or acceptable in quality or quantity.
adhere: To stick fast to a surface or substance.
adjacent: Next to or adjoining something else.
adjective: A word that describes a noun.
adjoin: To be next to and joined with.
adjust: To alter or move something slightly.
administer: To manage and be responsible for the running of something.
admire: To regard with respect or warm approval.
admit: To confess to be true or to allow entry.
adopt: To take up or start to use or follow.
adore: To regard with deep, often rapturous love.
adorn: To make more beautiful or attractive.
adult: A person who is fully grown or developed.
advance: To move forward in a purposeful way.
advert: To refer to in speaking or writing.
advice: Guidance or recommendations offered with regard to prudent future action.
advise: To offer suggestions about the best course of action.
aesthetic: Concerned with beauty or the appreciation of beauty.
affable: Friendly, good-natured, or easy to talk to.
affect: To have an effect on; make a difference to.
affection: A gentle feeling of fondness or liking.
affiliate: Officially attach or connect to an organization.
affirm: To state as a fact; assert strongly and publicly.
affix: To stick, attach, or fasten something to something else.
afflict: To cause pain or suffering to.
affluent: Having a great deal of money; wealthy.
afford: To have enough money to pay for.
agency: A business or organization providing a particular service.
agenda: A list of items to be discussed at a meeting.
aggravate: To make a problem or injury worse or more serious.
aggregate: A whole formed by combining several elements.
agile: Able to move quickly and easily.
agonize: To undergo great pain or struggle.
agree: To have the same opinion about something.
aim: To point or direct something at a target.
air: The invisible gaseous substance surrounding the earth.
aisle: A passage between rows of seats in a building.
album: A collection of recordings or photographs.
alert: Quick to notice and respond to potential danger.
alien: A foreigner or someone from another country.
allege: To claim or assert that someone has done something illegal.
allay: To diminish or put at rest (fear, suspicion, or worry).
allege: To claim or assert that someone has done something illegal.
alliance: A union or association formed for mutual benefit.
allocate: To distribute resources or duties for a particular purpose.
allude: To suggest or call attention to indirectly.
allure: The quality of being powerfully and mysteriously attractive.
allusion: An expression designed to call something to mind without mentioning it explicitly.
alluvial: Relating to or derived from alluvium (sediment deposited by flowing water).
ally: A state formally cooperating with another for a military or other purpose.
alone: Having no one else present; solitary.
along: Moving in a constant direction on or beside.
alter: To change or cause to change in character or composition.
alternative: Available as another possibility or choice.
although: In spite of the fact that.
altitude: The height of an object or point in relation to sea level.
altruism: The belief in or practice of selfless concern for the well-being of others.
ambiguous: Open to more than one interpretation.
ambition: A strong desire to do or achieve something.
ample: Enough or more than enough; plentiful.
amplify: To increase the volume of sound.
amputate: To cut off a limb or part of a limb.
analyze: To examine methodically and in detail.
ancient: Belonging to the very distant past.
anger: A strong feeling of displeasure or hostility.
angle: The space between two intersecting lines.
angry: Feeling or showing strong displeasure or hostility.
animal: A living organism that feeds on organic matter.
animate: To bring to life or make lively.
annex: To append or add as an extra or subordinate part.
annoy: To cause slight anger or irritation.
